{{Short description|Okinawan folkloric figures}} The '''Kijimuna''' ([[Okinawan language|Okinawan]]: {{lang|ryu|キジムナー}}, ''kijimunaa'', also ''kijimun'' 木の精<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://hougen.ajima.jp/e345|title = マジムン（まじむん）とは | 沖縄方言辞典 あじまぁ}}</ref>), or '''Bunagaya''', are creatures of the [[mythology]] native to the island of [[Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa]]. They are said to look around three or four years old and have red hair.<ref>Okinawan folktales- kijimuna. (2008, June 14). Retrieved from http://www.travelblog.org/Asia/Japan/Okinawa/Camp-Kinser/blog-287249.html</ref>
== About == The kijimuna are small wood spirits according to Okinawan mythology. The kijimuna are said to live in [[trees]], but the most common one is the 'gajumaru' or [[banyan]] tree. They are often described as being child-sized, with red hair covering their bodies and large heads. They are also known to be excellent fisherman, able to catch many [[fish]], but then only eating one of the eyes of the fish before leaving the rest of it. The [[Kijimuna Festival|Kijimuna festival]] in Okinawa is named after them. Another name for the kijimuna is "bunagaya," which means roughly "Large-Headed."<ref>Okinawan folktales. (n.d.). Retrieved from http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120305101207/http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html |date=2012-03-05 }}</ref> The Kijimuna are known to be very mischievous, playing pranks and tricking humans. One of their best-known tricks is to lie upon a person's chest, making them unable to move or breathe. This is known as "kanashibari."<ref>BIBLIOGRAPHIC CITATION: Morgan, T. (2007, January 16). Retrieved from {{cite web |url=http://www.travisjmorgan.com/blog/2007/01/16/kijimuna-okinawa-fairy-inkblot/ |title=Travis Morgan » Kijimuna (Okinawa Fairy) - Inkblot |access-date=2010-09-22 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080829134800/http://www.travisjmorgan.com/blog/2007/01/16/kijimuna-okinawa-fairy-inkblot/ |archive-date=2008-08-29 }} PARENTHETICAL CITATION: (Morgan, 2007)</ref> Even though the Kijimuna are tricksters, they have been known to make friends with humans. However, these relationships often go sour. A kijimuna may offer to carry a human on its back as it leaps through the mountains and over the seas. The kijimuna dislike people passing gas on their backs, however, and will immediately throw the human off, no matter where they were at the moment. The kijimuna also hate octopuses.<ref>Okinawan folktales. (n.d.). Retrieved from http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120305101207/http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html |date=2012-03-05 }}</ref>
==Stories== The kijimuna are a common subject in Okinawan folk tales. Many of their stories begin with the kijimuna becoming a human's friend and then ending with the relationship going bad. One story tells of a kijimuna's friend burning down his tree, so the kijimuna fled to the mountains.<ref>Okinawan folktales- kijimuna. (2008, June 14). Retrieved from http://www.travelblog.org/Asia/Japan/Okinawa/Camp-Kinser/blog-287249.html</ref>
== In popular culture == A Kijimuna appears as a recurring character in Disney's anime series ''[[Stitch!]]''. His special power is sneezing because his nose is powerful enough to blow anyone away.
In ''[[Doodle Champion Island Games|Google Doodle Champion Island Games]]'', The Kijimuna are described as having red hair and body and love racing on the beach.
Set in Okinawa, the anime ''[[The Aquatope on White Sand]]'' features a Kijimuna that appears frequently in earlier episodes. It is implied to be the source of visions experienced by visitors to the Gama Gama Aquarium.
==See also== * [[:ja:マジムン]] (majimun, or Okinawan equivalent of yōkai)

{{Short description|Okinawan folkloric figures}} The '''Kijimuna''' ([[Okinawan language|Okinawan]]: {{lang|ryu|キジムナー}}, ''kijimunaa'', also ''kijimun'' 木の精<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://hougen.ajima.jp/e345|title = マジムン（まじむん）とは | 沖縄方言辞典 あじまぁ}}</ref>), or '''Bunagaya''', are creatures of the [[mythology]] native to the island of [[Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa]]. They are said to look around three or four years old and have red hair.<ref>Okinawan folktales- kijimuna. (2008, June 14). Retrieved from http://www.travelblog.org/Asia/Japan/Okinawa/Camp-Kinser/blog-287249.html</ref>
== About == The kijimuna are small wood spirits according to Okinawan mythology. The kijimuna are said to live in [[trees]], but the most common one is the 'gajumaru' or [[banyan]] tree. They are often described as being child-sized, with red hair covering their bodies and large heads. They are also known to be excellent fisherman, able to catch many [[fish]], but then only eating one of the eyes of the fish before leaving the rest of it. The [[Kijimuna Festival|Kijimuna festival]] in Okinawa is named after them. Another name for the kijimuna is "bunagaya," which means roughly "Large-Headed."<ref>Okinawan folktales. (n.d.). Retrieved from http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120305101207/http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html |date=2012-03-05 }}</ref> The Kijimuna are known to be very mischievous, playing pranks and tricking humans. One of their best-known tricks is to lie upon a person's chest, making them unable to move or breathe. This is known as "kanashibari."<ref>BIBLIOGRAPHIC CITATION: Morgan, T. (2007, January 16). Retrieved from {{cite web |url=http://www.travisjmorgan.com/blog/2007/01/16/kijimuna-okinawa-fairy-inkblot/ |title=Travis Morgan » Kijimuna (Okinawa Fairy) - Inkblot |access-date=2010-09-22 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080829134800/http://www.travisjmorgan.com/blog/2007/01/16/kijimuna-okinawa-fairy-inkblot/ |archive-date=2008-08-29 }} PARENTHETICAL CITATION: (Morgan, 2007)</ref> Even though the Kijimuna are tricksters, they have been known to make friends with humans. However, these relationships often go sour. A kijimuna may offer to carry a human on its back as it leaps through the mountains and over the seas. The kijimuna dislike people passing gas on their backs, however, and will immediately throw the human off, no matter where they were at the moment. The kijimuna also hate octopuses.<ref>Okinawan folktales. (n.d.). Retrieved from http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120305101207/http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html |date=2012-03-05 }}</ref>
==Stories== The kijimuna are a common subject in Okinawan folk tales. Many of their stories begin with the kijimuna becoming a human's friend and then ending with the relationship going bad. One story tells of a kijimuna's friend burning down his tree, so the kijimuna fled to the mountains.<ref>Okinawan folktales- kijimuna. (2008, June 14). Retrieved from http://www.travelblog.org/Asia/Japan/Okinawa/Camp-Kinser/blog-287249.html</ref>
== In popular culture == A Kijimuna appears as a recurring character in Disney's anime series ''[[Stitch!]]''. His special power is sneezing because his nose is powerful enough to blow anyone away.
In ''[[Doodle Champion Island Games|Google Doodle Champion Island Games]]'', The Kijimuna are described as having red hair and body and love racing on the beach.
Set in Okinawa, the anime ''[[The Aquatope on White Sand]]'' features a Kijimuna that appears frequently in earlier episodes. It is implied to be the source of visions experienced by visitors to the Gama Gama Aquarium.
==See also== * [[:ja:マジムン]] (majimun, or Okinawan equivalent of yōkai)
==References== {{reflist}}
{{Japanese folklore long}}

The kijimuna dislike people passing gas on their backs, however, and will immediately throw the human off, no matter where they were at the moment. The kijimuna also hate octopuses.<ref>Okinawan folktales. (n.d.). Retrieved from http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120305101207/http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html |date=2012-03-05 }}</ref>
==Stories== The kijimuna are a common subject in Okinawan folk tales. Many of their stories begin with the kijimuna becoming a human's friend and then ending with the relationship going bad. One story tells of a kijimuna's friend burning down his tree, so the kijimuna fled to the mountains.<ref>Okinawan folktales- kijimuna. (2008, June 14). Retrieved from http://www.travelblog.org/Asia/Japan/Okinawa/Camp-Kinser/blog-287249.html</ref>
== In popular culture == A Kijimuna appears as a recurring character in Disney's anime series ''[[Stitch!]]''. His special power is sneezing because his nose is powerful enough to blow anyone away.
In ''[[Doodle Champion Island Games|Google Doodle Champion Island Games]]'', The Kijimuna are described as having red hair and body and love racing on the beach.
Set in Okinawa, the anime ''[[The Aquatope on White Sand]]'' features a Kijimuna that appears frequently in earlier episodes. It is implied to be the source of visions experienced by visitors to the Gama Gama Aquarium.

The kijimuna dislike people passing gas on their backs, however, and will immediately throw the human off, no matter where they were at the moment. The kijimuna also hate octopuses.<ref>Okinawan folktales. (n.d.). Retrieved from http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120305101207/http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html |date=2012-03-05 }}</ref>
==Stories== The kijimuna are a common subject in Okinawan folk tales. Many of their stories begin with the kijimuna becoming a human's friend and then ending with the relationship going bad. One story tells of a kijimuna's friend burning down his tree, so the kijimuna fled to the mountains.<ref>Okinawan folktales- kijimuna. (2008, June 14). Retrieved from http://www.travelblog.org/Asia/Japan/Okinawa/Camp-Kinser/blog-287249.html</ref>
== In popular culture == A Kijimuna appears as a recurring character in Disney's anime series ''[[Stitch!]]''. His special power is sneezing because his nose is powerful enough to blow anyone away.
In ''[[Doodle Champion Island Games|Google Doodle Champion Island Games]]'', The Kijimuna are described as having red hair and body and love racing on the beach.
Set in Okinawa, the anime ''[[The Aquatope on White Sand]]'' features a Kijimuna that appears frequently in earlier episodes. It is implied to be the source of visions experienced by visitors to the Gama Gama Aquarium.
==See also== * [[:ja:マジムン]] (majimun, or Okinawan equivalent of yōkai)

Retrieved from http://www.travelblog.org/Asia/Japan/Okinawa/Camp-Kinser/blog-287249.html</ref>
== About == The kijimuna are small wood spirits according to Okinawan mythology. The kijimuna are said to live in [[trees]], but the most common one is the 'gajumaru' or [[banyan]] tree. They are often described as being child-sized, with red hair covering their bodies and large heads. The kijimuna also hate octopuses.<ref>Okinawan folktales. (n.d.). revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{Short description|Okinawan folkloric figures}} The '''Kijimuna''' ([[Okinawan language|Okinawan]]: {{lang|ryu|キジムナー}}, ''kijimunaa'', also ''kijimun'' 木の精<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://hougen.ajima.jp/e345|title = マジムン（まじむん）とは | 沖縄方言辞典 あじまぁ}}</ref>), or '''Bunagaya''', are creatures of the [[mythology]] native to the island of [[Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa]]. They are said to look around three or four years old and have red hair.<ref>Okinawan folktales- kijimuna. (2008, June 14). Retrieved from http://www.travelblog.org/Asia/Japan/Okinawa/Camp-Kinser/blog-287249.html</ref>
== About == The kijimuna are small wood spirits according to Okinawan mythology. The kijimuna are said to live in [[trees]], but the most common one is the 'gajumaru' or [[:en:Banyan|Banyan]] tree. They are often described as being child-sized, with red hair covering their bodies and large heads. They are also known to be excellent fisherman, able to catch many [[fish]], but then only eating one of the eyes of the fish before leaving the rest of it. The [[Kijimuna Festival|Kijimuna festival]] in Okinawa is named after them. Another name for the kijimuna is "bunagaya," which means roughly "Large-Headed."<ref>Okinawan folktales. (n.d.). Retrieved from http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120305101207/http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html |date=2012-03-05 }}</ref> The Kijimuna are known to be very mischievous, playing pRanks and tricking humans. One of their best-known tricks is to lie upon a person's chest, making them unable to move or breathe. This is known as "kanashibari."<ref>BIBLIOGRAPHIC CITATION: Morgan, T. (2007, January 16). Retrieved from {{cite web |url=http://www.travisjmorgan.com/blog/2007/01/16/kijimuna-okinawa-fairy-inkblot/ |title=Travis Morgan » Kijimuna (Okinawa Fairy) - Inkblot |access-date=2010-09-22 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080829134800/http://www.travisjmorgan.com/blog/2007/01/16/kijimuna-okinawa-fairy-inkblot/ |archive-date=2008-08-29 }} PARENTHETICAL CITATION: (Morgan, 2007)</ref> Even though the Kijimuna are tricksters, they have been known to make friends with humans. However, these relationships often go sour. A kijimuna may offer to carry a human on its back as it leaps through the mountains and over the seas. The kijimuna dislike people passing gas on their backs, however, and will immediately throw the human off, no matter where they were at the moment. The kijimuna also hate octopuses.<ref>Okinawan folktales. (n.d.). Retrieved from http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120305101207/http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html |date=2012-03-05 }}</ref>
==Stories== The kijimuna are a common subject in Okinawan folk tales. Many of their stories begin with the kijimuna becoming a human's friend and then ending with the relationship going bad. One story tells of a kijimuna's friend burning down his tree, so the kijimuna fled to the mountains.<ref>Okinawan folktales- kijimuna. (2008, June 14). Retrieved from http://www.travelblog.org/Asia/Japan/Okinawa/Camp-Kinser/blog-287249.html</ref>
== In popular culture == A Kijimuna appears as a recurring character in Disney's anime series ''[[Stitch!]]''. His special power is sneezing because his nose is powerful enough to blow anyone away.
In ''[[Doodle Champion Island Games|Google Doodle Champion Island Games]]'', The Kijimuna are described as having red hair and body and love racing on the beach.
Set in Okinawa, the anime ''[[The Aquatope on White Sand]]'' features a Kijimuna that appears frequently in earlier episodes. It is implied to be the source of visions experienced by visitors to the Gama Gama Aquarium.
==See also== * [[:ja:マジムン]] (majimun, or Okinawan equivalent of yōkai)

== About == The kijimuna are small wood spirits according to Okinawan mythology. The kijimuna are said to live in [[trees]], but the most common one is the 'gajumaru' or [[:en:Banyan|Banyan]] tree. They are often described as being child-sized, with red hair covering their bodies and large heads. They are also known to be excellent fisherman, able to catch many [[:en:Fish|Fish]], but then only eating one of the eyes of the fish before leaving the rest of it. The [[:en:Kijimuna Festival|Kijimuna festival]] in Okinawa is named after them. Another name for the kijimuna is "bunagaya," which means roughly "Large-Headed."<ref>Okinawan folktales. (n.d.). Retrieved from http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120305101207/http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html |date=2012-03-05 }}</ref> The Kijimuna are known to be very mischievous, playing pRanks and tricking humans. One of their best-known tricks is to lie upon a person's chest, making them unable to move or breathe. This is known as "kanashibari."<ref>BIBLIOGRAPHIC CITATION: Morgan, T. (2007, January 16). Retrieved from {{cite web |url=http://www.travisjmorgan.com/blog/2007/01/16/kijimuna-okinawa-fairy-inkblot/ |title=Travis Morgan » Kijimuna (Okinawa Fairy) - Inkblot |access-date=2010-09-22 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080829134800/http://www.travisjmorgan.com/blog/2007/01/16/kijimuna-okinawa-fairy-inkblot/ |archive-date=2008-08-29 }} PARENTHETICAL CITATION: (Morgan, 2007)</ref> Even though the Kijimuna are tricksters, they have been known to make friends with humans. However, these relationships often go sour. A kijimuna may offer to carry a human on its back as it leaps through the mountains and over the seas. The kijimuna dislike people passing gas on their backs, however, and will immediately throw the human off, no matter where they were at the moment. The kijimuna also hate octopuses.<ref>Okinawan folktales. (n.d.). Retrieved from http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120305101207/http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html |date=2012-03-05 }}</ref>
==Stories== The kijimuna are a common subject in Okinawan folk tales. Many of their stories begin with the kijimuna becoming a human's friend and then ending with the relationship going bad. One story tells of a kijimuna's friend burning down his tree, so the kijimuna fled to the mountains.<ref>Okinawan folktales- kijimuna. (2008, June 14). Retrieved from http://www.travelblog.org/Asia/Japan/Okinawa/Camp-Kinser/blog-287249.html</ref>
== In popular culture == A Kijimuna appears as a recurring character in Disney's anime series ''[[Stitch!]]''. His special power is sneezing because his nose is powerful enough to blow anyone away.
In ''[[:en:Doodle Champion Island Games|Google Doodle Champion Island Games]]'', The Kijimuna are described as having red hair and body and love racing on the beach.
Set in Okinawa, the anime ''[[The Aquatope on White Sand]]'' features a Kijimuna that appears frequently in earlier episodes. It is implied to be the source of visions experienced by visitors to the Gama Gama Aquarium.
==See also== * [[:ja:マジムン]] (majimun, or Okinawan equivalent of yōkai)

One story tells of a kijimuna's friend burning down his tree, so the kijimuna fled to the mountains.<ref>Okinawan folktales- kijimuna. (2008, June 14). Retrieved from http://www.travelblog.org/Asia/Japan/Okinawa/Camp-Kinser/blog-287249.html</ref>

Retrieved from http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120305101207/http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html |date=2012-03-05 }}</ref> The Kijimuna are known to be very mischievous, playing pRanks and tricking humans. One of their best-known tricks is to lie upon a person's chest, making them unable to move or breathe. This is known as "kanashibari."<ref>BIBLIOGRAPHIC CITATION: Morgan, T. (2007, January 16). Retrieved from {{cite web |url=http://www.travisjmorgan.com/blog/2007/01/16/kijimuna-okinawa-fairy-inkblot/ |title=Travis Morgan » Kijimuna (Okinawa Fairy) - Inkblot |access-date=2010-09-22 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080829134800/http://www.travisjmorgan.com/blog/2007/01/16/kijimuna-okinawa-fairy-inkblot/ |archive-date=2008-08-29 }} PARENTHETICAL CITATION: (Morgan, 2007)</ref> Even though the Kijimuna are tricksters, they have been known to make friends with humans. However, these relationships often go sour. A kijimuna may offer to carry a human on its back as it leaps through the mountains and over the seas. The kijimuna dislike people passing gas on their backs, however, and will immediately throw the human off, no matter where they were at the moment. The kijimuna also hate octopuses.<ref>Okinawan folktales. (n.d.). Retrieved from http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120305101207/http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html |date=2012-03-05 }}</ref>
==Stories== The kijimuna are a common subject in Okinawan folk tales. Many of their stories begin with the kijimuna becoming a human's friend and then ending with the relationship going bad. One story tells of a kijimuna's friend burning down his tree, so the kijimuna fled to the mountains.<ref>Okinawan folktales- kijimuna. (2008, June 14). Retrieved from http://www.travelblog.org/Asia/Japan/Okinawa/Camp-Kinser/blog-287249.html</ref>
== In popular culture == A Kijimuna appears as a recurring character in Disney's anime series ''[[Stitch!]]''. His special power is sneezing because his nose is powerful enough to blow anyone away.
In ''[[:en:Doodle Champion Island Games|Google Doodle Champion Island Games]]'', The Kijimuna are described as having red hair and body and love racing on the beach.
Set in Okinawa, the anime ''[[:en:The Aquatope on White Sand|The Aquatope on White Sand]]'' features a Kijimuna that appears frequently in earlier episodes. It is implied to be the source of visions experienced by visitors to the Gama Gama Aquarium.
==See also== * [[:ja:マジムン]] (majimun, or Okinawan equivalent of yōkai)

{{Short description|Okinawan folkloric figures}} The '''Kijimuna''' ([[:en:Okinawan language|Okinawan]]: {{lang|ryu|キジムナー}}, ''kijimunaa'', also ''kijimun'' 木の精<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://hougen.ajima.jp/e345|title = マジムン（まじむん）とは | 沖縄方言辞典 あじまぁ}}</ref>), or '''Bunagaya''', are creatures of the [[:en:Myth|Myth]] native to the island of [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa]]. They are said to look around three or four years old and have red hair.<ref>Okinawan folktales- kijimuna. (2008, June 14). Retrieved from http://www.travelblog.org/Asia/Japan/Okinawa/Camp-Kinser/blog-287249.html</ref>
== About == The kijimuna are small wood spirits according to Okinawan mythology. The kijimuna are said to live in [[trees]], but the most common one is the 'gajumaru' or [[:en:Banyan|Banyan]] tree. They are often described as being child-sized, with red hair covering their bodies and large heads. They are also known to be excellent fisherman, able to catch many [[:en:Fish|Fish]], but then only eating one of the eyes of the fish before leaving the rest of it. The [[:en:Kijimuna Festival|Kijimuna festival]] in Okinawa is named after them. Another name for the kijimuna is "bunagaya," which means roughly "Large-Headed."<ref>Okinawan folktales. (n.d.). Retrieved from http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120305101207/http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html |date=2012-03-05 }}</ref> The Kijimuna are known to be very mischievous, playing pRanks and tricking humans. One of their best-known tricks is to lie upon a person's chest, making them unable to move or breathe. This is known as "kanashibari."<ref>BIBLIOGRAPHIC CITATION: Morgan, T. (2007, January 16). Retrieved from {{cite web |url=http://www.travisjmorgan.com/blog/2007/01/16/kijimuna-okinawa-fairy-inkblot/ |title=Travis Morgan » Kijimuna (Okinawa Fairy) - Inkblot |access-date=2010-09-22 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080829134800/http://www.travisjmorgan.com/blog/2007/01/16/kijimuna-okinawa-fairy-inkblot/ |archive-date=2008-08-29 }} PARENTHETICAL CITATION: (Morgan, 2007)</ref> Even though the Kijimuna are tricksters, they have been known to make friends with humans. However, these relationships often go sour. A kijimuna may offer to carry a human on its back as it leaps through the mountains and over the seas. The kijimuna dislike people passing gas on their backs, however, and will immediately throw the human off, no matter where they were at the moment. The kijimuna also hate octopuses.<ref>Okinawan folktales. (n.d.). Retrieved from http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120305101207/http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html |date=2012-03-05 }}</ref>
==Stories== The kijimuna are a common subject in Okinawan folk tales. Many of their stories begin with the kijimuna becoming a human's friend and then ending with the relationship going bad. One story tells of a kijimuna's friend burning down his tree, so the kijimuna fled to the mountains.<ref>Okinawan folktales- kijimuna. (2008, June 14). Retrieved from http://www.travelblog.org/Asia/Japan/Okinawa/Camp-Kinser/blog-287249.html</ref>
== In popular culture == A Kijimuna appears as a recurring character in Disney's anime series ''[[:en:Stitch!|Stitch!]]''. His special power is sneezing because his nose is powerful enough to blow anyone away.
In ''[[:en:Doodle Champion Island Games|Google Doodle Champion Island Games]]'', The Kijimuna are described as having red hair and body and love racing on the beach.
Set in Okinawa, the anime ''[[:en:The Aquatope on White Sand|The Aquatope on White Sand]]'' features a Kijimuna that appears frequently in earlier episodes. It is implied to be the source of visions experienced by visitors to the Gama Gama Aquarium.
==See also== * [[:ja:マジムン]] (majimun, or Okinawan equivalent of yōkai)

They are often described as being child-sized, with red hair covering their bodies and large heads. They are also known to be excellent fisherman, able to catch many [[:en:Fish|Fish]], but then only eating one of the eyes of the fish before leaving the rest of it. The [[:en:Kijimuna Festival|Kijimuna festival]] in Okinawa is named after them. Another name for the kijimuna is "bunagaya," which means roughly "Large-Headed."<ref>Okinawan folktales. (n.d.). Retrieved from http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120305101207/http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html |date=2012-03-05 }}</ref> The Kijimuna are known to be very mischievous, playing pRanks and tricking humans. One of their best-known tricks is to lie upon a person's chest, making them unable to move or breathe. This is known as "kanashibari."<ref>BIBLIOGRAPHIC CITATION: Morgan, T. (2007, January 16). Retrieved from {{cite web |url=http://www.travisjmorgan.com/blog/2007/01/16/kijimuna-okinawa-fairy-inkblot/ |title=Travis Morgan » Kijimuna (Okinawa Fairy) - Inkblot |access-date=2010-09-22 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080829134800/http://www.travisjmorgan.com/blog/2007/01/16/kijimuna-okinawa-fairy-inkblot/ |archive-date=2008-08-29 }} PARENTHETICAL CITATION: (Morgan, 2007)</ref> Even though the Kijimuna are tricksters, they have been known to make friends with humans. However, these relationships often go sour. A kijimuna may offer to carry a human on its back as it leaps through the mountains and over the seas. The kijimuna dislike people passing gas on their backs, however, and will immediately throw the human off, no matter where they were at the moment. The kijimuna also hate octopuses.<ref>Okinawan folktales. (n.d.). Retrieved from http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120305101207/http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html |date=2012-03-05 }}</ref>
==Stories== The kijimuna are a common subject in Okinawan folk tales. Many of their stories begin with the kijimuna becoming a human's friend and then ending with the relationship going bad. One story tells of a kijimuna's friend burning down his tree, so the kijimuna fled to the mountains.<ref>Okinawan folktales- kijimuna. (2008, June 14). Retrieved from http://www.travelblog.org/Asia/Japan/Okinawa/Camp-Kinser/blog-287249.html</ref>
== In popular culture == A Kijimuna appears as a recurring character in Disney's anime series ''[[:en:Stitch!|Stitch!]]''. His special power is sneezing because his nose is powerful enough to blow anyone away.
In ''[[:en:Doodle Champion Island Games|Google Doodle Champion Island Games]]'', The Kijimuna are described as having red hair and body and love racing on the beach.
Set in Okinawa, the anime ''[[:en:The Aquatope on White Sand|The Aquatope on White Sand]]'' features a Kijimuna that appears frequently in earlier episodes. It is implied to be the source of visions experienced by visitors to the Gama Gama Aquarium.
==See also== * [[:ja:マジムン]] (majimun, or Okinawan equivalent of yōkai)

In ''[[:en:Doodle Champion Island Games|Google Doodle Champion Island Games]]'', The Kijimuna are described as having red hair and body and love racing on the beach.
Set in Okinawa, the anime ''[[:en:The Aquatope on White Sand|The Aquatope on White Sand]]'' features a Kijimuna that appears frequently in earlier episodes. It is implied to be the source of visions experienced by visitors to the Gama Gama Aquarium.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kijimuna]][[es:Kijimuna]][[fr:Kijimuna]][[id:Kijimunaa]][[ja:キジムナー]][[pt:Kijimuna]][[ru:Кидзимуна]][[zh-yue:木精]][[zh:木之精]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q93651]] Wikidata name: Kijimuna [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q93651]] Wikidata name: Kijimuna [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Okinawan folkloric figures}} The '''Kijimuna''' ([[:en:Okinawan language|Okinawan]]: {{lang|ryu|キジムナー}}, ''kijimunaa'', also ''kijimun'' 木の精<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://hougen.ajima.jp/e345|title = マジムン（まじむん）とは | 沖縄方言辞典 あじまぁ}}</ref>), or '''Bunagaya''', are creatures of the [[:en:Myth|Myth]] native to the island of [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa]]. They are said to look around three or four years old and have red hair.<ref>Okinawan folktales- kijimuna. (2008, June 14). Retrieved from http://www.travelblog.org/Asia/Japan/Okinawa/Camp-Kinser/blog-287249.html</ref>
== About == The kijimuna are small wood spirits according to Okinawan mythology. The kijimuna are said to live in [[:en:trees|trees]], but the most common one is the 'gajumaru' or [[:en:Banyan|Banyan]] tree. They are often described as being child-sized, with red hair covering their bodies and large heads. They are also known to be excellent fisherman, able to catch many [[:en:Fish|Fish]], but then only eating one of the eyes of the fish before leaving the rest of it. The [[:en:Kijimuna Festival|Kijimuna festival]] in Okinawa is named after them. Another name for the kijimuna is "bunagaya," which means roughly "Large-Headed."<ref>Okinawan folktales. (n.d.). Retrieved from http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120305101207/http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html |date=2012-03-05 }}</ref> The Kijimuna are known to be very mischievous, playing pRanks and tricking humans. One of their best-known tricks is to lie upon a person's chest, making them unable to move or breathe. This is known as "kanashibari."<ref>BIBLIOGRAPHIC CITATION: Morgan, T. (2007, January 16). Retrieved from {{cite web |url=http://www.travisjmorgan.com/blog/2007/01/16/kijimuna-okinawa-fairy-inkblot/ |title=Travis Morgan » Kijimuna (Okinawa Fairy) - Inkblot |access-date=2010-09-22 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080829134800/http://www.travisjmorgan.com/blog/2007/01/16/kijimuna-okinawa-fairy-inkblot/ |archive-date=2008-08-29 }} PARENTHETICAL CITATION: (Morgan, 2007)</ref> Even though the Kijimuna are tricksters, they have been known to make friends with humans. However, these relationships often go sour. A kijimuna may offer to carry a human on its back as it leaps through the mountains and over the seas. The kijimuna dislike people passing gas on their backs, however, and will immediately throw the human off, no matter where they were at the moment. The kijimuna also hate octopuses.<ref>Okinawan folktales. (n.d.). Retrieved from http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120305101207/http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html |date=2012-03-05 }}</ref>
==Stories== The kijimuna are a common subject in Okinawan folk tales. Many of their stories begin with the kijimuna becoming a human's friend and then ending with the relationship going bad. One story tells of a kijimuna's friend burning down his tree, so the kijimuna fled to the mountains.<ref>Okinawan folktales- kijimuna. (2008, June 14). Retrieved from http://www.travelblog.org/Asia/Japan/Okinawa/Camp-Kinser/blog-287249.html</ref>
== In popular culture == A Kijimuna appears as a recurring character in Disney's anime series ''[[:en:Stitch!|Stitch!]]''. His special power is sneezing because his nose is powerful enough to blow anyone away.
In ''[[:en:Doodle Champion Island Games|Google Doodle Champion Island Games]]'', The Kijimuna are described as having red hair and body and love racing on the beach.
Set in Okinawa, the anime ''[[:en:The Aquatope on White Sand|The Aquatope on White Sand]]'' features a Kijimuna that appears frequently in earlier episodes. It is implied to be the source of visions experienced by visitors to the Gama Gama Aquarium.
==See also== * [[:ja:マジムン]] (majimun, or Okinawan equivalent of yōkai)

Retrieved from http://www.travelblog.org/Asia/Japan/Okinawa/Camp-Kinser/blog-287249.html</ref>
== About == The kijimuna are small wood spirits according to Okinawan mythology. The kijimuna are said to live in [[:en:trees|trees]], but the most common one is the 'gajumaru' or [[:en:Banyan|Banyan]] tree. They are often described as being child-sized, with red hair covering their bodies and large heads. They are also known to be excellent fisherman, able to catch many [[:en:Fish|Fish]], but then only eating one of the eyes of the fish before leaving the rest of it. The [[:en:Kijimuna Festival|Kijimuna festival]] in Okinawa is named after them. Another name for the kijimuna is "bunagaya," which means roughly "Large-Headed."<ref>Okinawan folktales. (n.d.). Retrieved from http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120305101207/http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html |date=2012-03-05 }}</ref> The Kijimuna are known to be very mischievous, playing pRanks and tricking humans. One of their best-known tricks is to lie upon a person's chest, making them unable to move or breathe. This is known as "kanashibari."<ref>BIBLIOGRAPHIC CITATION: Morgan, T. (2007, January 16). Retrieved from {{cite web |url=http://www.travisjmorgan.com/blog/2007/01/16/kijimuna-okinawa-fairy-inkblot/ |title=Travis Morgan » Kijimuna (Okinawa Fairy) - Inkblot |access-date=2010-09-22 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080829134800/http://www.travisjmorgan.com/blog/2007/01/16/kijimuna-okinawa-fairy-inkblot/ |archive-date=2008-08-29 }} PARENTHETICAL CITATION: (Morgan, 2007)</ref> Even though the Kijimuna are tricksters, they have been known to make friends with humans. However, these relationships often go sour. A kijimuna may offer to carry a human on its back as it leaps through the mountains and over the seas. The kijimuna dislike people passing gas on their backs, however, and will immediately throw the human off, no matter where they were at the moment. The kijimuna also hate octopuses.<ref>Okinawan folktales. (n.d.). Retrieved from http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120305101207/http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html |date=2012-03-05 }}</ref>
==Stories== The kijimuna are a common subject in Okinawan folk tales. Many of their stories begin with the kijimuna becoming a human's friend and then ending with the relationship going bad. One story tells of a kijimuna's friend burning down his tree, so the kijimuna fled to the mountains.<ref>Okinawan folktales- kijimuna. (2008, June 14). Retrieved from http://www.travelblog.org/Asia/Japan/Okinawa/Camp-Kinser/blog-287249.html</ref>
== In popular culture == A Kijimuna appears as a recurring character in Disney's anime series ''[[:en:Stitch!|Stitch!]]''. His special power is sneezing because his nose is powerful enough to blow anyone away.
In ''[[:en:Doodle Champion Island Games|Google Doodle Champion Island Games]]'', The Kijimuna are described as having red hair and body and love racing on the beach.
Set in Okinawa, the anime ''[[:en:The Aquatope on White Sand|The Aquatope on White Sand]]'' features a Kijimuna that appears frequently in earlier episodes. It is implied to be the source of visions experienced by visitors to the Gama Gama Aquarium.
==See also== * [[:ja:マジムン]] (majimun, or Okinawan equivalent of yōkai)

One of their best-known tricks is to lie upon a person's chest, making them unable to move or breathe. This is known as "kanashibari."<ref>BIBLIOGRAPHIC CITATION: Morgan, T. (2007, January 16). Retrieved from {{cite web |url=http://www.travisjmorgan.com/blog/2007/01/16/kijimuna-okinawa-fairy-inkblot/ |title=Travis Morgan » Kijimuna (Okinawa Fairy) - Inkblot |access-date=2010-09-22 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080829134800/http://www.travisjmorgan.com/blog/2007/01/16/kijimuna-okinawa-fairy-inkblot/ |archive-date=2008-08-29 }} PARENTHETICAL CITATION: (Morgan, 2007)</ref> Even though the Kijimuna are tricksters, they have been known to make friends with humans. However, these relationships often go sour. A kijimuna may offer to carry a human on its back as it leaps through the mountains and over the seas. The kijimuna dislike people passing gas on their backs, however, and will immediately throw the human off, no matter where they were at the moment. The kijimuna also hate octopuses.<ref>Okinawan folktales. (n.d.). Retrieved from http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120305101207/http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html |date=2012-03-05 }}</ref>
==Stories== The kijimuna are a common subject in Okinawan folk tales. Many of their stories begin with the kijimuna becoming a human's friend and then ending with the relationship going bad. One story tells of a kijimuna's friend burning down his tree, so the kijimuna fled to the mountains.<ref>Okinawan folktales- kijimuna. (2008, June 14). Retrieved from http://www.travelblog.org/Asia/Japan/Okinawa/Camp-Kinser/blog-287249.html</ref>
== In popular culture == A Kijimuna appears as a recurring character in Disney's anime series ''[[:en:Stitch!|Stitch!]]''. His special power is sneezing because his nose is powerful enough to blow anyone away.
In ''[[:en:Doodle Champion Island Games|Google Doodle Champion Island Games]]'', The Kijimuna are described as having red hair and body and love racing on the beach.
Set in Okinawa, the anime ''[[:en:The Aquatope on White Sand|The Aquatope on White Sand]]'' features a Kijimuna that appears frequently in earlier episodes. It is implied to be the source of visions experienced by visitors to the Gama Gama Aquarium.
==See also== * [[:ja:マジムン]] (majimun, or Okinawan equivalent of yōkai)

They are also known to be excellent fisherman, able to catch many [[:en:Fish|Fish]], but then only eating one of the eyes of the fish before leaving the rest of it. The [[:en:Kijimuna Festival|Kijimuna festival]] in Okinawa is named after them. Another name for the kijimuna is "bunagaya," which means roughly "Large-Headed."<ref>Okinawan folktales. (n.d.). Retrieved from http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120305101207/http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html |date=2012-03-05 }}</ref> The Kijimuna are known to be very mischievous, playing pRanks and tricking humans. One of their best-known tricks is to lie upon a person's chest, making them unable to move or breathe. This is known as "kanashibari."<ref>BIBLIOGRAPHIC CITATION: Morgan, T. (2007, January 16). Retrieved from {{cite web |url=http://www.travisjmorgan.com/blog/2007/01/16/kijimuna-okinawa-fairy-inkblot/ |title=Travis Morgan » Kijimuna (Okinawa Fairy) - Inkblot |access-date=2010-09-22 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080829134800/http://www.travisjmorgan.com/blog/2007/01/16/kijimuna-okinawa-fairy-inkblot/ |archive-date=2008-08-29 }} PARENTHETICAL CITATION: (Morgan, 2007)</ref> Even though the Kijimuna are tricksters, they have been known to make friends with humans. However, these relationships often go sour. A kijimuna may offer to carry a human on its back as it leaps through the mountains and over the seas. The kijimuna dislike people passing gas on their backs, however, and will immediately throw the human off, no matter where they were at the moment. The kijimuna also hate octopuses.<ref>Okinawan folktales. (n.d.). Retrieved from http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120305101207/http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html |date=2012-03-05 }}</ref>
==Stories== The kijimuna are a common subject in Okinawan folk tales. Many of their stories begin with the kijimuna becoming a human's friend and then ending with the relationship going bad. One story tells of a kijimuna's friend burning down his tree, so the kijimuna fled to the mountains.<ref>Okinawan folktales- kijimuna. (2008, June 14). Retrieved from http://www.travelblog.org/Asia/Japan/Okinawa/Camp-Kinser/blog-287249.html</ref>
== In popular culture == A Kijimuna appears as a recurring character in Disney's anime series ''[[:en:Stitch!|Stitch!]]''. His special power is sneezing because his nose is powerful enough to blow anyone away.
In ''[[:en:Doodle Champion Island Games|Google Doodle Champion Island Games]]'', The Kijimuna are described as having red hair and body and love racing on the beach.
Set in Okinawa, the anime ''[[:en:The Aquatope on White Sand|The Aquatope on White Sand]]'' features a Kijimuna that appears frequently in earlier episodes. It is implied to be the source of visions experienced by visitors to the Gama Gama Aquarium.
==See also== * [[:ja:マジムン]] (majimun, or Okinawan equivalent of yōkai)

They are also known to be excellent fisherman, able to catch many {{ill|Fish|en|Fish|WD=Q152|lt=Fish}}, but then only eating one of the eyes of the fish before leaving the rest of it. The {{ill|Kijimuna Festival|en|Kijimuna Festival|WD=Q6406163|lt=Kijimuna festival}} in Okinawa is named after them. Another name for the kijimuna is "bunagaya," which means roughly "Large-Headed."<ref>Okinawan folktales. (n.d.). Retrieved from http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120305101207/http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html |date=2012-03-05 }}</ref> The Kijimuna are known to be very mischievous, playing pRanks and tricking humans. One of their best-known tricks is to lie upon a person's chest, making them unable to move or breathe. This is known as "kanashibari."<ref>BIBLIOGRAPHIC CITATION: Morgan, T. (2007, January 16). However, these relationships often go sour. A kijimuna may offer to carry a human on its back as it leaps through the mountains and over the seas. The kijimuna dislike people passing gas on their backs, however, and will immediately throw the human off, no matter where they were at the moment. The kijimuna also hate octopuses.<ref>Okinawan folktales. (n.d.). Retrieved from http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120305101207/http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html |date=2012-03-05 }}</ref>

One story tells of a kijimuna's friend burning down his tree, so the kijimuna fled to the mountains.<ref>Okinawan folktales- kijimuna. (2008, June 14). Retrieved from http://www.travelblog.org/Asia/Japan/Okinawa/Camp-Kinser/blog-287249.html</ref>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kijimuna]][[es:Kijimuna]][[fr:Kijimuna]][[id:Kijimunaa]][[ja:キジムナー]][[pt:Kijimuna]][[ru:Кидзимуна]][[zh-yue:木精]][[zh:木之精]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kijimuna]][[es:Kijimuna]][[fr:Kijimuna]][[id:Kijimunaa]][[ja:キジムナー]][[pt:Kijimuna]][[ru:Кидзимуна]][[zh-yue:木精]][[zh:木之精]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kijimuna]][[es:Kijimuna]][[fr:Kijimuna]][[id:Kijimunaa]][[ja:キジムナー]][[pt:Kijimuna]][[ru:Кидзимуна]][[zh-yue:木精]][[zh:木之精]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q93651]] Wikidata name: Kijimuna [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q93651]] Wikidata name: Kijimuna [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Okinawan folkloric figures}} The '''Kijimuna''' ({{ill|Okinawan language|en|Okinawan language|WD=Q34233|lt=Okinawan}}: {{lang|ryu|キジムナー}}, ''kijimunaa'', also ''kijimun'' 木の精<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://hougen.ajima.jp/e345|title = マジムン（まじむん）とは | 沖縄方言辞典 あじまぁ}}</ref>), or '''Bunagaya''', are creatures of the {{ill|Myth|en|Myth|WD=Q12827256|lt=Myth}} native to the island of {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa}}. They are said to look around three or four years old and have red hair.<ref>Okinawan folktales- kijimuna. (2008, June 14). Retrieved from http://www.travelblog.org/Asia/Japan/Okinawa/Camp-Kinser/blog-287249.html</ref>
== About == The kijimuna are small wood spirits according to Okinawan mythology. The kijimuna are said to live in [[:en:trees|trees]], but the most common one is the 'gajumaru' or {{ill|Banyan|en|Banyan|WD=Q5678886|lt=Banyan}} tree. They are often described as being child-sized, with red hair covering their bodies and large heads. They are also known to be excellent fisherman, able to catch many {{ill|Fish|en|Fish|WD=Q152|lt=Fish}}, but then only eating one of the eyes of the fish before leaving the rest of it. The {{ill|Kijimuna Festival|en|Kijimuna Festival|WD=Q6406163|lt=Kijimuna festival}} in Okinawa is named after them. Another name for the kijimuna is "bunagaya," which means roughly "Large-Headed."<ref>Okinawan folktales. (n.d.). Retrieved from http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120305101207/http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html |date=2012-03-05 }}</ref> The Kijimuna are known to be very mischievous, playing pRanks and tricking humans. One of their best-known tricks is to lie upon a person's chest, making them unable to move or breathe. This is known as "kanashibari."<ref>BIBLIOGRAPHIC CITATION: Morgan, T. (2007, January 16). Retrieved from {{cite web |url=http://www.travisjmorgan.com/blog/2007/01/16/kijimuna-okinawa-fairy-inkblot/ |title=Travis Morgan » Kijimuna (Okinawa Fairy) - Inkblot |access-date=2010-09-22 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080829134800/http://www.travisjmorgan.com/blog/2007/01/16/kijimuna-okinawa-fairy-inkblot/ |archive-date=2008-08-29 }} PARENTHETICAL CITATION: (Morgan, 2007)</ref> Even though the Kijimuna are tricksters, they have been known to make friends with humans. However, these relationships often go sour. A kijimuna may offer to carry a human on its back as it leaps through the mountains and over the seas. The kijimuna dislike people passing gas on their backs, however, and will immediately throw the human off, no matter where they were at the moment. The kijimuna also hate octopuses.<ref>Okinawan folktales. (n.d.). Retrieved from http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120305101207/http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html |date=2012-03-05 }}</ref>
==Stories== The kijimuna are a common subject in Okinawan folk tales. Many of their stories begin with the kijimuna becoming a human's friend and then ending with the relationship going bad. One story tells of a kijimuna's friend burning down his tree, so the kijimuna fled to the mountains.<ref>Okinawan folktales- kijimuna. (2008, June 14). Retrieved from http://www.travelblog.org/Asia/Japan/Okinawa/Camp-Kinser/blog-287249.html</ref>
== In popular culture == A Kijimuna appears as a recurring character in Disney's anime series ''{{ill|Stitch!|en|Stitch!|WD=Q1151166|lt=Stitch!}}''. His special power is sneezing because his nose is powerful enough to blow anyone away.
In ''{{ill|Doodle Champion Island Games|en|Doodle Champion Island Games|WD=Q107627602|lt=Google Doodle Champion Island Games}}'', The Kijimuna are described as having red hair and body and love racing on the beach.
Set in Okinawa, the anime ''{{ill|The Aquatope on White Sand|en|The Aquatope on White Sand|WD=Q104843562|lt=The Aquatope on White Sand}}'' features a Kijimuna that appears frequently in earlier episodes. It is implied to be the source of visions experienced by visitors to the Gama Gama Aquarium.
==See also== * [[:ja:マジムン]] (majimun, or Okinawan equivalent of yōkai)

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kijimuna]][[es:Kijimuna]][[fr:Kijimuna]][[id:Kijimunaa]][[ja:キジムナー]][[pt:Kijimuna]][[ru:Кидзимуна]][[zh-yue:木精]][[zh:木之精]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kijimuna]][[es:Kijimuna]][[fr:Kijimuna]][[id:Kijimunaa]][[ja:キジムナー]][[pt:Kijimuna]][[ru:Кидзимуна]][[zh-yue:木精]][[zh:木之精]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kijimuna]][[es:Kijimuna]][[fr:Kijimuna]][[id:Kijimunaa]][[ja:キジムナー]][[pt:Kijimuna]][[ru:Кидзимуна]][[zh-yue:木精]][[zh:木之精]]
{{Short description|Okinawan folkloric figures}} The '''Kijimuna''' ({{ill|Okinawan language|en|Okinawan language|WD=Q34233|lt=Okinawan|1=Okinawan language}}: {{lang|ryu|キジムナー}}, ''kijimunaa'', also ''kijimun'' 木の精<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://hougen.ajima.jp/e345|title = マジムン（まじむん）とは | 沖縄方言辞典 あじまぁ}}</ref>), or '''Bunagaya''', are creatures of the {{ill|Myth|en|Myth|WD=Q12827256|lt=Myth|1=Myth}} native to the island of {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa|1=Okinawa Prefecture}}. They are said to look around three or four years old and have red hair.<ref>Okinawan folktales- kijimuna. (2008, June 14). Retrieved from http://www.travelblog.org/Asia/Japan/Okinawa/Camp-Kinser/blog-287249.html</ref>
== About == The kijimuna are small wood spirits according to Okinawan mythology. The kijimuna are said to live in [[:en:trees|trees]], but the most common one is the 'gajumaru' or {{ill|Banyan|en|Banyan|WD=Q5678886|lt=Banyan|1=Banyan}} tree. They are often described as being child-sized, with red hair covering their bodies and large heads. They are also known to be excellent fisherman, able to catch many {{ill|Fish|en|Fish|WD=Q152|lt=Fish|1=Fish}}, but then only eating one of the eyes of the fish before leaving the rest of it. The {{ill|Kijimuna Festival|en|Kijimuna Festival|WD=Q6406163|lt=Kijimuna festival|1=Kijimuna Festival}} in Okinawa is named after them. Another name for the kijimuna is "bunagaya," which means roughly "Large-Headed."<ref>Okinawan folktales. (n.d.). Retrieved from http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120305101207/http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html |date=2012-03-05 }}</ref> The Kijimuna are known to be very mischievous, playing pRanks and tricking humans. One of their best-known tricks is to lie upon a person's chest, making them unable to move or breathe. This is known as "kanashibari."<ref>BIBLIOGRAPHIC CITATION: Morgan, T. (2007, January 16). Retrieved from {{cite web |url=http://www.travisjmorgan.com/blog/2007/01/16/kijimuna-okinawa-fairy-inkblot/ |title=Travis Morgan » Kijimuna (Okinawa Fairy) - Inkblot |access-date=2010-09-22 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080829134800/http://www.travisjmorgan.com/blog/2007/01/16/kijimuna-okinawa-fairy-inkblot/ |archive-date=2008-08-29 }} PARENTHETICAL CITATION: (Morgan, 2007)</ref> Even though the Kijimuna are tricksters, they have been known to make friends with humans. However, these relationships often go sour. A kijimuna may offer to carry a human on its back as it leaps through the mountains and over the seas. The kijimuna dislike people passing gas on their backs, however, and will immediately throw the human off, no matter where they were at the moment. The kijimuna also hate octopuses.<ref>Okinawan folktales. (n.d.). Retrieved from http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120305101207/http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html |date=2012-03-05 }}</ref>
==Stories== The kijimuna are a common subject in Okinawan folk tales. Many of their stories begin with the kijimuna becoming a human's friend and then ending with the relationship going bad. One story tells of a kijimuna's friend burning down his tree, so the kijimuna fled to the mountains.<ref>Okinawan folktales- kijimuna. (2008, June 14). Retrieved from http://www.travelblog.org/Asia/Japan/Okinawa/Camp-Kinser/blog-287249.html</ref>
== In popular culture == A Kijimuna appears as a recurring character in Disney's anime series ''{{ill|Stitch!|en|Stitch!|WD=Q1151166|lt=Stitch!|1=Stitch!}}''. His special power is sneezing because his nose is powerful enough to blow anyone away.
In ''{{ill|Doodle Champion Island Games|en|Doodle Champion Island Games|WD=Q107627602|lt=Google Doodle Champion Island Games|1=Doodle Champion Island Games}}'', The Kijimuna are described as having red hair and body and love racing on the beach.
Set in Okinawa, the anime ''{{ill|The Aquatope on White Sand|en|The Aquatope on White Sand|WD=Q104843562|lt=The Aquatope on White Sand|1=The Aquatope on White Sand}}'' features a Kijimuna that appears frequently in earlier episodes. It is implied to be the source of visions experienced by visitors to the Gama Gama Aquarium.
==See also== * [[:ja:マジムン]] (majimun, or Okinawan equivalent of yōkai)

revision.text:
{{Short description|Okinawan folkloric figures}} The '''Kijimuna''' ({{ill|Okinawan language|en|Okinawan language|qid=Q34233|lt=Okinawan|1=Okinawan language}}: {{lang|ryu|キジムナー}}, ''kijimunaa'', also ''kijimun'' 木の精<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://hougen.ajima.jp/e345|title = マジムン（まじむん）とは | 沖縄方言辞典 あじまぁ}}</ref>), or '''Bunagaya''', are creatures of the {{ill|Myth|en|Myth|qid=Q12827256|lt=Myth|1=Myth}} native to the island of {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|qid=Q766445|lt=Okinawa|1=Okinawa Prefecture}}. They are said to look around three or four years old and have red hair.<ref>Okinawan folktales- kijimuna. (2008, June 14). Retrieved from http://www.travelblog.org/Asia/Japan/Okinawa/Camp-Kinser/blog-287249.html</ref>
== About == The kijimuna are small wood spirits according to Okinawan mythology. The kijimuna are said to live in [[:en:trees|trees]], but the most common one is the 'gajumaru' or {{ill|Banyan|en|Banyan|qid=Q5678886|lt=Banyan|1=Banyan}} tree. They are often described as being child-sized, with red hair covering their bodies and large heads. They are also known to be excellent fisherman, able to catch many {{ill|Fish|en|Fish|qid=Q152|lt=Fish|1=Fish}}, but then only eating one of the eyes of the fish before leaving the rest of it. The {{ill|Kijimuna Festival|en|Kijimuna Festival|qid=Q6406163|lt=Kijimuna festival|1=Kijimuna Festival}} in Okinawa is named after them. Another name for the kijimuna is "bunagaya," which means roughly "Large-Headed."<ref>Okinawan folktales. (n.d.). Retrieved from http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120305101207/http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html |date=2012-03-05 }}</ref> The Kijimuna are known to be very mischievous, playing pRanks and tricking humans. One of their best-known tricks is to lie upon a person's chest, making them unable to move or breathe. This is known as "kanashibari."<ref>BIBLIOGRAPHIC CITATION: Morgan, T. (2007, January 16). Retrieved from {{cite web |url=http://www.travisjmorgan.com/blog/2007/01/16/kijimuna-okinawa-fairy-inkblot/ |title=Travis Morgan » Kijimuna (Okinawa Fairy) - Inkblot |access-date=2010-09-22 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080829134800/http://www.travisjmorgan.com/blog/2007/01/16/kijimuna-okinawa-fairy-inkblot/ |archive-date=2008-08-29 }} PARENTHETICAL CITATION: (Morgan, 2007)</ref> Even though the Kijimuna are tricksters, they have been known to make friends with humans. However, these relationships often go sour. A kijimuna may offer to carry a human on its back as it leaps through the mountains and over the seas. The kijimuna dislike people passing gas on their backs, however, and will immediately throw the human off, no matter where they were at the moment. The kijimuna also hate octopuses.<ref>Okinawan folktales. (n.d.). Retrieved from http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120305101207/http://www.chicagookinawakenjinkai.com/Folklore%20pages/Kijimuna.html |date=2012-03-05 }}</ref>
==Stories== The kijimuna are a common subject in Okinawan folk tales. Many of their stories begin with the kijimuna becoming a human's friend and then ending with the relationship going bad. One story tells of a kijimuna's friend burning down his tree, so the kijimuna fled to the mountains.<ref>Okinawan folktales- kijimuna. (2008, June 14). Retrieved from http://www.travelblog.org/Asia/Japan/Okinawa/Camp-Kinser/blog-287249.html</ref>
== In popular culture == A Kijimuna appears as a recurring character in Disney's anime series ''{{ill|Stitch!|en|Stitch!|qid=Q1151166|lt=Stitch!|1=Stitch!}}''. His special power is sneezing because his nose is powerful enough to blow anyone away.
In ''{{ill|Doodle Champion Island Games|en|Doodle Champion Island Games|qid=Q107627602|lt=Google Doodle Champion Island Games|1=Doodle Champion Island Games}}'', The Kijimuna are described as having red hair and body and love racing on the beach.
Set in Okinawa, the anime ''{{ill|The Aquatope on White Sand|en|The Aquatope on White Sand|qid=Q104843562|lt=The Aquatope on White Sand|1=The Aquatope on White Sand}}'' features a Kijimuna that appears frequently in earlier episodes. It is implied to be the source of visions experienced by visitors to the Gama Gama Aquarium.
